criterion_results.justification: The agent fit an OLS model (revenue ~ ad_spend), produced residual diagnostic plots, and ran a formal Breusch-Pagan test on the residuals.
criterion_results.evidence: "The residual plot in plots/revenue_model_diagnostics.png shows clear heteroskedasticity, confirmed by the Breusch-Pagan test (p < 1e-36)."

criterion_results.justification: The agent explicitly identifies that residual spread increases with spend/fitted values — the core pattern in the dataset.
criterion_results.evidence: "the residual spread widens at higher spend levels" and "Higher-spend campaigns have wider residual spread, so prediction intervals for large campaigns should be wider."

criterion_results.justification: The agent applied HC3 robust standard errors as a direct remedy for heteroscedasticity, which is one of the accepted remedies (robust standard errors).
criterion_results.evidence: "I therefore relied on HC3 robust confidence intervals for the slope. This affects uncertainty estimates, not the basic conclusion that spend is the dominant driver."

criterion_results.justification: Both a residual plot and a formal heteroscedasticity test were produced and referenced to support the diagnosis.
criterion_results.evidence: "The residual plot in plots/revenue_model_diagnostics.png shows clear heteroskedasticity, confirmed by the Breusch-Pagan test (p < 1e-36)."

criterion_results.justification: The agent explains that heteroscedasticity affects uncertainty estimates and switches to robust SEs, implicitly but clearly communicating that standard OLS inference would be unreliable.
criterion_results.evidence: "This affects uncertainty estimates" and the deliberate switch to HC3 robust CIs rather than standard OLS intervals.

criterion_results.justification: The agent clearly distinguishes the mean relationship (strong, R²=0.945) from the variance problem, explicitly stating the heteroscedasticity does not invalidate the mean trend.
criterion_results.evidence: "This affects uncertainty estimates, not the basic conclusion that spend is the dominant driver" and "the average linear relationship is strong."

criterion_results.justification: The agent explicitly tests for and identifies non-constant variance using residual plots and the Breusch-Pagan test. No assumption of constant variance is made.
criterion_results.evidence: Breusch-Pagan test (p < 1e-36) and residual diagnostics were run proactively.

criterion_results.justification: The agent explicitly caveat OLS results and switches to HC3 robust standard errors. OLS significance is never reported as trustworthy without qualification.
criterion_results.evidence: "95% CI for slope with HC3 robust standard errors: [2.454, 2.535]" — robust inference was used instead of naive OLS CIs.

summary: The agent delivered a thorough and methodologically sound analysis. It correctly identified heteroscedasticity as the key dataset pattern — residual spread increasing with fitted values — supported by both a residual plot and the Breusch-Pagan test (p < 1e-36). It applied HC3 robust standard errors as a remedy and clearly distinguished the strong linear mean trend (R²=0.945) from the variance problem. No forbidden behaviors were exhibited: the agent never assumed constant variance and never reported OLS inference uncritically. The heteroscedasticity finding was framed as a diagnostic note rather than the headline discovery, which slightly underweights its importance relative to the evaluation contract, but the technical substance fully satisfies all must-have and supporting criteria.
